evidence: All 15+ internal API routes consistently use validateInternalSecret() from lib/internal-auth.ts with timing-safe comparison (crypto.timingSafeEqual)
All v1 agent-facing routes consistently use authenticateAgentRequest() from lib/agent-auth.ts
All user-facing routes under /api/servers/** consistently use getServerSession(authOptions) — 20 files verified
Bootstrap routes use authenticateAdminToken() with CSRF protection and timing-safe comparison
route-handlers.js lines 47 and 710 use simple === comparison for INTERNAL_API_SECRET instead of timing-safe validateInternalSecret(), contradicting ISSUE-010 fix
GET /api/v1/agents/[id] is intentionally unauthenticated (documented as public agent info in route-handlers.js line 529)
Webhook routes correctly use token-in-URL as auth (documented pattern matching Discord), with rate limiting
Permission checks use shared constants from lib/permissions.ts (Permissions.MANAGE_MESSAGES etc.), not magic strings
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: route-handlers.js uses plain === for internal secret validation in two handler factories, undermining the timing-safe comparison established by ISSUE-010 across the rest of the internal API surface

strengths: Three distinct auth strategies (internal-secret, agent-key, session) are cleanly separated into dedicated modules (internal-auth.ts, agent-auth.ts, admin-auth.ts) and used consistently across their respective route families
Permission checks use a well-designed bitfield system with shared constants — no magic permission strings anywhere in the codebase
Internal API routes uniformly apply validateInternalSecret() as the first operation in every handler (15+ routes verified), making the pattern easy to audit
Admin endpoints (bootstrap) include defense-in-depth: admin token + first-run guard + rate limiting + CSRF protection via Origin header rejection
authorization_consistency.issue_character: A single file (route-handlers.js) retains a pre-ISSUE-010 pattern for internal secret validation, creating a localized inconsistency in an otherwise uniformly applied auth boundary.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong auth consistency across three distinct auth domains — internal, agent, and user session — with clean module separation and no magic strings. The one defect (timing-unsafe comparison in route-handlers.js) is localized to a single file with two instances, but it affects a critical path (message persistence) and directly contradicts the project's own ISSUE-010 fix. This pulls the score below 90 but the overall posture remains solid, placing it around 84-85.

issues.summary: route-handlers.js uses plain === for INTERNAL_API_SECRET, bypassing timing-safe validateInternalSecret()
evidence: route-handlers.js line 47: `if (secret !== process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET)` in createInternalMessagesPostHandler — simple string equality
route-handlers.js line 710: `if (secret !== process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET)` in createAgentVerifyHandler — same pattern
internal-auth.ts provides validateInternalSecret() using crypto.timingSafeEqual, created specifically to fix ISSUE-010 timing side-channel
All 15+ other internal route handlers correctly use validateInternalSecret() — this file is the sole outlier
issues.suggestion: Import and use validateInternalSecret from @/lib/internal-auth in both createInternalMessagesPostHandler and createAgentVerifyHandler, replacing the direct === comparison. Since route-handlers.js is a .js file, either convert it to .ts or use require/dynamic import to access the function.

root_causes: route-handlers.js was written before ISSUE-010 introduced timing-safe validation and was not updated when the fix was rolled out to other internal routes
